md: **Thales' Theorem** is one of the oldest named theorems in mathematics, attributed to Thales of Miletus (~600 BC). It says:
**If you draw a triangle inside a circle with one side as the diameter, the angle opposite that diameter is a right angle.**
Equivalently: any inscribed angle that subtends a diameter is $90┬░$. Drag $P$ around the circle below ŌĆö the angle at $P$ is always exactly $90┬░$, no matter where $P$ goes.

blocks.title: Why this works ŌĆö two ways to see it
blocks.body:
**Inscribed angle reasoning.** Diameter $AB$ subtends a *straight angle* (180┬░) at the center. By the Inscribed Angle Theorem, the inscribed angle is half of that = $90┬░$. Done.

**Isosceles reasoning.** Connect $P$ to the center $O$. Then $OA = OP = OB$ are all radii. So $\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$ are both isosceles. Let $\angle OAP = \alpha$ and $\angle OBP = \beta$. By Base Angles, $\angle OPA = \alpha$ and $\angle OPB = \beta$. Triangle $APB$ has angles $\alpha, \beta, (\alpha + \beta)$ summing to $180┬░$, so $\alpha + \beta = 90┬░$. That's $\angle APB$.ŌłÄ

md: **If $\angle APB = 90┬░$, then $P$ lies on a circle with $AB$ as diameter.**

Why is this useful? Because it gives you a powerful construction: given any segment $AB$, the set of points $P$ that see $AB$ at a right angle is exactly the circle with diameter $AB$ (minus $A$ and $B$ themselves).
This is the **Thales' circle** of segment $AB$. It's used constantly in compass-and-straightedge constructions and proofs.
$AB$ is a diameter of a circle of radius 10. Point $P$ is on the circle with $AP = 12$. Find $BP$.
**By Thales:** $\angle APB = 90┬░$. So $\triangle APB$ is a right triangle with hypotenuse $AB = 20$ (the diameter).
Sanity check: 12-16-20 is a 3-4-5 right triangle scaled by 4. Ō£ō

blocks.title: Applications
blocks.body:
**Constructing a right angle without a protractor.** Given any segment, draw its perpendicular bisector to find the midpoint. Draw a semicircle with the segment as diameter. Any point on the arc gives you a right angle.

**Tangent from external point.** To draw the tangent from an external point $E$ to a circle with center $O$: draw circle with diameter $OE$, find where it meets the original circle ŌĆö those points $T$ satisfy $\angle OTE = 90┬░$ (Thales), which is the tangent condition.

**Locus problems.** 'Find all points seeing $AB$ at $90┬░$' has a clean answer: the Thales circle of $AB$.
